glossary.definition: A retry strategy where you wait longer and longer between each attempt after an error, preventing you from overwhelming the server.
A small amount of randomness added to your wait times so that multiple programs do not accidentally retry at the exact same millisecond.
A cheaper, slower way to send thousands of requests to an AI provider all at once, useful for large jobs that do not need instant answers.

hook.scenario: You launch your RAG pipeline for a batch job processing 5,000 documents. Within 30 seconds you start getting 429 errors. Your naive retry loop hammers the API faster, getting more 429s, and the job grinds to a halt.
hook.question: How do you handle rate limits without making them worse — and how do you write retry logic that actually works?

mental_model.intuition: A rate limit is a traffic light. Hammering the accelerator while the light is red doesn't make it green faster — you just waste fuel and block the intersection.
mental_model.description: LLM APIs enforce two kinds of limits: Requests Per Minute (RPM) and Tokens Per Minute (TPM). Hit either one and you get a 429 response. The correct response is exponential backoff — wait longer after each failure, add jitter (randomness) so your retries don't all fire at the same moment. The OpenAI SDK has built-in retry logic; in most cases you configure it rather than write it from scratch.

sections.body: Two limits apply simultaneously: RPM (max requests per minute) and TPM (max tokens per minute, counting prompt + completion). Your tier on the OpenAI platform determines both. A 429 error tells you which limit you hit in the error body. Tokens-per-minute is the more common hit in RAG systems with large contexts — not raw request count.
sections.recap: Two limits: RPM and TPM. RAG systems usually hit TPM first due to large prompt contexts.

sections.body: Exponential backoff with jitter is the correct retry strategy. After a 429: wait 1s, retry. If it fails again: wait 2s, retry. Then 4s, 8s — doubling each time, up to a max of ~60s. The jitter (random ±10–30% on the wait time) staggers parallel workers so they don't all retry at the same millisecond, which would just recreate the spike.
sections.recap: Backoff: double the wait each retry, cap at ~60s. Add jitter to prevent thundering herd.

sections.body: The openai Python SDK includes built-in retry with exponential backoff via the max_retries parameter. Setting max_retries=3 at client initialization means the SDK automatically retries on 429 and 5xx, with its own backoff logic — no manual code needed for simple cases. For fine-grained control (custom backoff, circuit breakers, per-request overrides) use the tenacity library.
sections.recap: Use OpenAI SDK's built-in max_retries for simple cases. Use tenacity for production-grade control.

sections.body: For bulk jobs (batch processing thousands of documents), the better solution is the Batch API. It accepts up to 50,000 requests in a JSONL file, processes them asynchronously over 24 hours, and costs 50% less per token. No rate limit errors — it queues work internally. Use the synchronous API only for real-time, user-facing requests where latency matters.
sections.recap: Batch API for offline bulk work: 50% cheaper, no rate limits, async. Sync API only for real-time.

code_snippets.title: Built-in SDK retry — the simplest fix
code_snippets.code:
from openai import OpenAI

# max_retries applies to 429 and 5xx — SDK handles backoff automatically
client = OpenAI(max_retries=3)

# Now every call through this client automatically retries up to 3 times
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o-mini',
    messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': 'Summarise this doc.'}],
    max_tokens=200,
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)
code_snippets.explanation: Setting max_retries=3 on the client is the zero-boilerplate fix for transient 429s and server errors. The SDK uses exponential backoff internally. This handles 90% of rate-limit situations in real-time pipelines.

code_snippets.title: Manual exponential backoff with jitter (for fine-grained control)
code_snippets.code:
import time
import random
from openai import OpenAI, RateLimitError

client = OpenAI(max_retries=0)  # disable SDK retry — we handle it ourselves

def call_with_backoff(messages: list[dict], max_retries=5) -> str:
    wait = 1.0
    for attempt in range(max_retries):
        try:
            resp = client.chat.completions.create(
                model='gpt-4o-mini',
                messages=messages,
                max_tokens=300,
            )
            return resp.choices[0].message.content
        except RateLimitError:
            if attempt == max_retries - 1:
                raise
            jitter = random.uniform(0.8, 1.2)   # ±20% jitter
            sleep_time = min(wait * jitter, 60)  # cap at 60s
            print(f'Rate limited. Waiting {sleep_time:.1f}s (attempt {attempt + 1})')
            time.sleep(sleep_time)
            wait *= 2                             # exponential backoff
    raise RuntimeError('Max retries exceeded')
code_snippets.explanation: Jitter (random multiplier) staggers retries from parallel workers — without it, 50 workers hitting a rate limit will all retry simultaneously, creating a thundering herd that triggers another wave of 429s. The cap at 60s prevents infinitely long waits.

code_snippets.title: Batch API for bulk offline processing (50% cheaper, no rate limits)
code_snippets.code:
import json
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()

# Step 1: Create a JSONL file of requests
requests = [
    {'custom_id': f'doc-{i}', 'method': 'POST',
     'url': '/v1/chat/completions',
     'body': {'model': 'gpt-4o-mini',
              'messages': [{'role': 'user', 'content': f'Summarise document {i}'}],
              'max_tokens': 200}}
    for i in range(1000)
]

with open('batch_input.jsonl', 'w') as f:
    for req in requests:
        f.write(json.dumps(req) + '\n')

# Step 2: Upload and submit the batch
batch_file = client.files.create(file=open('batch_input.jsonl', 'rb'), purpose='batch')
batch = client.batches.create(
    input_file_id=batch_file.id,
    endpoint='/v1/chat/completions',
    completion_window='24h',
)
print(f'Batch submitted: {batch.id} — poll status, results ready within 24h')
code_snippets.explanation: The Batch API processes requests asynchronously at 50% of the synchronous price. No 429 errors — it manages its own queue. Ideal for nightly pipelines, bulk embeddings, and offline evaluation runs. Poll batch.status until it is 'completed', then download results by output_file_id.

common_mistakes.title: Retrying immediately without backoff
common_mistakes.explanation: A tight retry loop (while True: try: call()) after a 429 will send even more requests per second, making the rate limit worse. Always wait before retrying — even a flat 1-second delay is better than a tight loop.

common_mistakes.title: Using the synchronous API for bulk batch jobs
common_mistakes.explanation: Processing 10,000 documents synchronously hits rate limits, ties up threads, and costs 2× what the Batch API would. Any job that doesn't need results in real time should use the Batch API.

recall_questions.q: What are the two types of rate limits enforced by the OpenAI API?
recall_questions.answer: Requests Per Minute (RPM) and Tokens Per Minute (TPM). Both count prompt + completion tokens. Hitting either triggers a 429.

recall_questions.q: What is exponential backoff with jitter and why is the jitter part important?
recall_questions.answer: Doubling the wait time after each failed retry (1s, 2s, 4s...), with random variation (jitter). Jitter staggers retries across parallel workers to prevent a 'thundering herd' — all workers retrying simultaneously and triggering another wave of 429s.

recall_questions.q: What is the main advantage of the OpenAI Batch API over the synchronous API for bulk jobs?
recall_questions.answer: 50% cheaper per token, no rate limit errors (it queues internally), and asynchronous — submit 50k requests at once and retrieve results within 24 hours.

oa_questions.question: Your bulk document-processing job fails after processing 200 of 5,000 documents with repeated 429 errors. Your retry logic retries immediately on each failure. What is wrong and how do you fix it?
oa_questions.answer: Immediate retries amplify the rate limit problem — each retry is another request against the same depleted quota. Fix: 1) implement exponential backoff with jitter so retries are spaced out. 2) Better: switch to the Batch API — submit all 5,000 as a batch, get results asynchronously at 50% cost with no rate limit errors.
oa_questions.approach: Two fixes at different levels: tactical (backoff for synchronous API) and strategic (Batch API for bulk offline work). The Batch API is the correct tool for this use case.

oa_questions.question: Two identical services both use gpt-4o-mini with max_retries=3. Service A uses a shared client singleton across all threads. Service B creates a new client per request. Under load (100 req/s), Service A gets far more 429s than Service B. Why?
oa_questions.answer: Service A uses a shared client, so all 100 requests per second count against the same RPM/TPM bucket. Service B creates a new client per request — but this doesn't help with server-side rate limits since they're keyed on the API key, not the client instance. Actually Service A and B would have the same 429 rate. This is a trick question: 429s are server-side per API key, not per client instance. The fix is request throttling or Batch API, not client isolation.
oa_questions.approach: Identify the trick: rate limits are server-side per API key, not per client object. Client isolation doesn't reduce 429s. Server-side throttling (token bucket, Batch API) is the real fix.
